message.content: Adversary-in-the-Middle: ARP Cache Poisoning involves adversaries manipulating the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) caches to intercept communications between networked devices. This technique exploits the stateless and unauthenticated nature of ARP, allowing adversaries to deceive devices into storing incorrect MAC addresses. By responding faster than the legitimate device or sending gratuitous ARP replies, adversaries can position themselves between communicating devices, enabling activities like Network Sniffing or Transmitted Data Manipulation. The impact includes potential interception and collection of sensitive data, such as credentials transmitted over unencrypted protocols. Common exploitation methods include passive waiting for ARP requests and sending malicious ARP replies. Mitigation strategies involve using static ARP entries, enabling ARP spoofing detection tools, employing encrypted communication protocols, and segmenting networks to limit ARP traffic exposure.
